insights.headline: Which months looked heavier than usual for each house?
insights.lede: 9 safehouses and 450 house-months in this export. Each month is scored against that site’s own recent pattern: whether this month’s incident pattern is heavier than that house’s own recent usual (rolling benchmark), not a fixed cutoff for all sites.

prediction_cards.label: Safehouses in file
prediction_cards.definition: Distinct sites and monthly observations in this export.

prediction_cards.label: How often the label fired
prediction_cards.hint: Share of months marked heavier than house benchmark (or positive momentum target)
prediction_cards.definition: Baseline rate of the outcome the score is trained to approximate.

prediction_cards.label: Technical quality
prediction_cards.hint: Holdout and grouped checks are in the notebook export.
prediction_cards.definition: ROC-AUC / CV metrics are omitted here; open safehouse-operational-load-risk.ipynb if you need them.

cause_cards.title: House vs its own history
cause_cards.body: Benchmarks roll within each safehouse so a busy site is not punished for being busier than a quiet one.
cause_cards.definition: Rolling medians and lags are computed per safehouse_id.

ranking_bullets: Houses are sorted by average load pressure score over the months you select: lower usually means steadier, calmer months in the data; higher means more months looked hot or rising compared with each site’s own past.
Use the list to decide where to dig in first—not as a final grade. Population, staffing, and data quality still belong in supervision.
benchmark_bullets: A month marked “heavier than usual” means that site’s incident pattern that month was above its own rolling benchmark from earlier months—not “bad compared to other cities.”
Load pressure score (0–100%) is how strongly this month’s signals resemble past months that were flagged heavy for that same house.
signal_bullets: The tool pays the most attention to: recent three-month incident pace (compared with earlier months at the same house), change in incidents-per-resident vs the prior month, last month’s incidents per resident. Together that means recent workload and momentum matter more than a single raw number in isolation.
Why this is not blame: a higher score can reflect tougher cases, staffing gaps, or reporting practices—not only performance. Use rankings to ask questions, not to label a site good or bad.
plain_answers.method_note: Built from safehouse-month rows: incidents, residents, visits, sessions, and lagged rolling rates. Patterns are learned from your export; they show association in history, not proof that one factor caused another.
